Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I'd end up binge-watching videos at weird hours when anxiety hit, then not touch anything for days. With 17 online courses, I was spread so thin that organizing felt impossible. I mostly just reacted to whatever felt most urgent rather than following any real plan.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly based on deadlines and honestly, my mental state. Some weeks I'd put in crazy hours—like 35 hours crammed into a few days before something was due. Other weeks I'd barely open anything because I was overwhelmed or dealing with extracurriculars. There was no consistency; I was always playing catch-up.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in. I'd get that sinking feeling that I'd missed something important or a deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was still passing or to see if anyone had posted something I needed to respond to. It was rarely because I was excited to learn—more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. There were brief periods where I'd check it daily, usually when I was scared about falling behind. But those phases never lasted. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped me manage anxiety, but they didn't make me more consistent with the platform itself.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched over 115 minutes of them because I could actually absorb information that way. Discussion posts were also helpful because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different perspectives. Anything visual or interactive worked better than walls of text.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just felt overwhelming, and I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but never did. The optional quizzes I mostly skipped because they felt like extra work when I was already drowning. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course or understanding something I was confused about. If I could tell it would help my exam score or assignment, I'd engage. But if it felt like busy work or extra content, my brain just couldn't justify spending energy on it when I had 16 other courses demanding attention.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones carried this weight of fear—like if I didn't do them, I'd fail. Optional stuff, even if interesting, got mentally categorized as 'nice to have' and then forgotten. There was always something required that I was behind on, so optional never made the cut.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study from my tablet offline, especially when I was commuting or didn't want to deal with spotty internet. I also used YouTube videos and external resources to understand concepts better. A lot of my actual learning happened with materials the LMS couldn't track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing downloaded notes or rewatching saved videos on my phone while on the bus or during breaks from my extracurriculars. I'd also study with classmates in person, working through problems together. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it was real studying.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then spend hours with it offline over the next few days. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times. It was more convenient than logging in repeatedly, especially when I was already stressed about everything.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I had assignments piling up across multiple courses, extracurricular commitments were intense, and I started feeling genuinely scared about failing. That fear paradoxically made it harder to engage—I'd avoid the LMS because logging in meant confronting how behind I was. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit with that spiral.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was constant with my workload, and confusion about what was actually important across 17 courses made me freeze up. When I was exhausted, I'd log in, stare at the dashboard, feel overwhelmed, and log out without doing anything meaningful. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also ate into study time constantly.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open while scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about other deadlines or just too tired to process anything. I'd watch videos without really absorbing them, just going through the motions. It felt like engagement but was really just presence without learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they mattered. The fear of not doing them well enough made starting feel impossible. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, which never came. By the time I'd start, I was rushing and couldn't do my best work. It's why my assignment completion was so low despite decent scores when I did submit.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly spend hours on the LMS cramming. Without that external pressure, I'd drift. My entire engagement pattern was deadline-driven rather than consistent. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned.
Q17: When I got good feedback on an assignment, it motivated me briefly—made me feel like maybe I could do this. But negative feedback or low scores made me want to avoid the course entirely. It's complicated because I needed the feedback but sometimes couldn't handle it emotionally, especially when I was already stressed.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across modules. I couldn't always tell what was essential versus supplementary, which made prioritizing hard. When structure was clear and linear, I engaged more. When it felt scattered or self-directed, I got lost and disengaged.
Q19: Clear deadlines and visual content made engagement easier. What made it harder was having too many resources without guidance on what mattered most. Also, when discussion posts were required but felt forced, I'd participate minimally just to check the box. The lack of clear pathways through the material was a real barrier.
Q20: That was me sometimes. I'd log in frequently out of anxiety, click through materials, watch parts of videos, participate in discussions—but then not submit the actual assignments. The fear of not doing them perfectly, combined with running out of time, meant activity didn't translate to output. It's engagement without completion.
Q21: That also kind of describes me. My exam score was decent because I'm good at cramming and I studied a lot offline. The LMS didn't capture my real preparation. Some people just learn differently or use external resources more effectively. Low activity doesn't mean low effort—it might mean effort happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, surface-level engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone is actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture fear-driven clicking versus genuine curiosity.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and absorbing the material, not just showing up digitally. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, connecting concepts to things I know. Real engagement is mental investment, which is different from just logging hours. I was rarely truly engaged this semester—mostly just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope even if it didn't change my login patterns. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline, the peer study sessions, or how my extracurriculars taught me things that connected to coursework. It definitely wouldn't show that I passed despite everything working against me.
